repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
fix https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/26575

# Problems
1. Can't get individual parts of a key-chord separately: modifiers, key 
2. Can't separate a key-combo into individual key-chords
<!---
## Other Uses
* `vim.on_key()` event value is the internal-key, so it could be un-parsed with `keytrans()` and then parse into individual parts...
--->
# Solution
Have a function which returns each key-chord separated into individual keys with the following information:
+ `key_raw` the key-chord (solving problem 2)
+ `mod` the modifiers of `key_raw` (solving problem 1)
+ `key_orig` the key part of the key-chord, only here if differing from `key` (this doesn't solve any of the above mentioned problems, but it may provide useful and it's (in terms of code) free)
+ `key` a normalized version of `key_orig` (solving problem 1), example(the first is `key_orig` and second is `key`): `lt` and `<`, `Bar` and `|` (in `<C-Bar>`)

comment_data.body: Shouldn't this be in `vim.keymap.parse()`? (Also, docstring/description please!)

comment_data.body:
Well, that depends on what the function does -- it wasn't exactly well documented before...

At a guess (correct me if I misunderstand what it does), I'd expand on the functionality in the general docstring and then just distinguish between the _targets_  in the parameters. (It's important not to use two different words for the same thing...)

Maybe something like
```
Converts a string of names of |keycodes| to different representations. By default, it returns a string with the internal representation of the actual keypress events, but it can also parse it into a table of base keys and modifiers.

    Example: >lua
        vim.g.mapleader = vim.keycode('<bs>')
        print(vim.g.mapleader)
        --- "<80>kb"
        
        vim.print(vim.keycode('<C-A><C-S-A>', { output = 'table' })
        --- { <insert output here> }
<
```
(@gpanders please correct the terminal terminology here)

And any optional arguments should not be positional for future extensions, so make this `opts` with `opts.output: 'table'|'code'?` to leave the door open for future formats.

(Naming TBD @justinmk)

+      • {parse}  (`boolean`) Parse the keycode instead.

+		  "alt_key" alternative representation of key, only present
+		  when it differs from "key"
+		  "orig_key" the original key with modifiers
+		  "mod" a list of single character modifiers of the key

comment_data.body: ternary
why is this in `src/nvim/message_defs.h` ?

there is precedent for this in `maparg()`, but can't find other cases.

Lua callers might be annoyed by the return-type polymorphism, though that in theory can be solved when luals/emmyls become more advanced.

anyway, to avoid the annoyance for Lua callers, we could have `vim.keycode()` return the `keytran()` dict as its 2nd retval?

Because it's `str2special()` which does half of the parsing, and that function is defined in `message.c`

is it intended to be understood that the list is an ordered sequence of key chords? if so, let's make that clearer.
```suggestion

```

comment_data.body:
why is this needed? i was expecting `vim.keycode()` to call `vim.fn.keytrans()`

oh, you reverted the `maparg()` change... hmm.

It is set when a character has an equivalent key-notation (e.x. `<` and `lt`).

Also for uppercase ASCII letters (which automatically are converted to lower case and the shift modifier is added), but the alt remains the upper case letter.

Also also when characters are passed to `transchar()`(c function)...

comment_data.body:
would you mind putting this in a new test file, `editor/keycode_spec.lua`

later i will move the `nvim_replace_termcodes` tests in there too.
this is the only test case that gives a hint about what `key_orig` looks like. are there some unusual cases worth testing?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3298,3 +3262,58 @@ describe('vim.fn.exists()', function()
     eq(0, fn.exists('*v:lua.?'))
   end)
 end)
+
+describe('vim.keycode() detailed info', function()
+  local function parse(key, simplify)
+    local parsed = exec_lua([[return select(2,vim.keycode(...,true))]], key)
+    return vim.tbl_map(function(tbl)
+      if simplify == 1 then
+        return { tbl.mod, tbl.key }
+      elseif simplify == 2 then
+        return { tbl.mod, tbl.key, tbl.key_raw }
+      end
+      return tbl
+    end, parsed)
+  end
+
+  it('works', function()
+    eq({ { mod = { 'M' }, key = 'f', key_raw = '<M-f>' } }, parse('<A-f>'))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, 'Home' } }, parse('<A-Home>', 1))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, 'f' }, { {}, 'b' }, { {}, 'c' } }, parse('<A-f>b<char-99>', 1))
+
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, '>' } }, parse('<A->>', 1))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, 't_>>' } }, parse('<A-t_>>>', 1))
+  end)
+
+  it('normalizes', function()
+    -- stylua: ignore start
+    eq({ { {},      '\\',   '\\'         } }, parse('\\', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, '\\',   '<M-Bslash>' } }, parse('<A-\\>', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'C' }, '\\',   '<C-\\>'     } }, parse('<C-\\>', 2))
+    eq({ { {},      '|',    '|'          } }, parse('|', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, '|',    '<M-Bar>'    } }, parse('<A-|>', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'C' }, '|',    '<C-Bar>'    } }, parse('<C-|>', 2))
+    eq({ { {},      '\127', '\127'       } }, parse('\127', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'M' }, '\127', '<M-^?>'     } }, parse('<A-\127>', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'C' }, '\127', '<C-^?>'     } }, parse('<C-\127>', 2))
+
+    eq({ { {}, '<',    '<lt>'    } }, parse('<', 2))
+    eq({ { {}, '\t',   '<Tab>'   } }, parse('\t', 2))
+    eq({ { {}, '\r',   '<CR>'    } }, parse('\r', 2))
+    eq({ { {}, '\n',   '<NL>'    } }, parse('\n', 2))
+    eq({ { {}, '\27', '<Esc>'   } }, parse('\27', 2))
+    eq({ { {}, ' ',    '<Space>' } }, parse(' ', 2))
+
+    eq({ { { 'S' }, 'a', 'A'     } }, parse('A', 2))
+    eq({ { { 'C' }, 'a', '<C-A>' } }, parse('<C-a>', 2))
+    -- stylua: ignore end
+
+    eq({ { mod = {}, key = '<', key_raw = '<lt>', key_orig = 'lt' } }, parse('<'))

comment_data.body: what is `_small_key` and does it have underscore prefix
these docstrings are nice. it would be great if `keycode_data` had docstrings like this: concise, give useful hints, and are in the universe with us
`key_orig` is just a slice of `key_raw`, e.g. (in case when <>) `"<"..concat(mod,"-").."-"..key_orig..">"` == `key_raw`, so the tests above (which use `key_raw`) indirectly include `key_orig`.

comment_data.body:
It's the value of `keytrans(vim.keycode(...))` separated to individual keys.
The use case here is when you want individual full keys of a keycombo for mapping purposes or similar.
comment_data.diff_hunk:

comment_data.body:
It's for storing data for {key}: typically {key} would be a slice of {key_raw}, but in the case of normalization, {key} would no longer be a slice of {key_raw}, so it's instead a one wide slice of {_small_key}.

Example, given `<A-|>`, {key_raw} would be `<M-Bar>` and {key} would need to be `|`, but as `|` is not in {key_raw}, {_small_key} is set to `|` and {key} is then taken a slice of {_small_key}.

I would say that the example two lines below that is given about when `{key_orig}`(which is described as non-normalized version of `{key}`) differs from `{key}` is enough (or is it not clear enough what that example is for?).

comment_regular_data.body:
Can the tests do a "roundtrip" using `fn.keytrans()`, or something like that, to double-check each assertion?

> The reason for doing it trough lua rather than using c with the already existing functions, is that through c, much more complexity would be needed to implement the feature.

Are you sure? We have about 5+ related functions already, so want to be certain that we need yet another implementation. 

Why can't [`replace_termcodes`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/0a3add979a2689843673301044a14b3484d9d96f/src/nvim/keycodes.c#L663) optionally build a API `Object` ? I would not expect that to severely complicate it.

title: feat: keymap parse
